title: Nullable Types
description: How Kotlin actively prevents your app from crashing.

slides.title: Strict by Default 🚫
slides.content:
## No Nulls Allowed

By default, Kotlin variables strictly **cannot** hold `null`. 

If you define `var name: String = "Alice"`, and then later try to write `name = null`, the Kotlin compiler will throw a nasty red error and absolutely refuse to even build your app.

slides.title: The Question Mark ❓
slides.content:
But what if a variable legitimately *needs* to be capable of holding nothing? (e.g., A user hasn't uploaded a profile picture yet).

You must explicitly declare the data type as **Nullable** by appending a question mark `?` to the end of the type.

quizData.question: Which of these variable definitions explicitly allows the variable to hold a `null` value?
quizData.explanation: Appending the strict `?` to the DataType (e.g., `Int?`) signals to the compiler that this specialized box is permitted to sit empty.

slides.title: The Trade-off ⚖️
slides.content:
## With Great Power...

If you declare a variable as Nullable (e.g. `String?`), Kotlin will immediately lock that variable down.

You can no longer safely call methods on it (like `.length`), because Kotlin is terrified the variable might currently be empty and cause a crash!

quizData.question: If `var word: String? = null`, why won't Kotlin let you directly call `word.length`?

options.text: Because it would crash the program if it executed while empty.
Because the variable is locked by a password.

quizData.explanation: Kotlin intentionally blocks compilation to save you. It refuses to let you risk a fatal NullPointerException. You must prove it is safe first!
